name: FABS
what: Premise/support indicator quartet — For, After all, Because, Since. When you see one, what comes after it is evidence; the conclusion is right next door.

name: 2-Part Conclusion Check
what: Verification protocol. (1) Sounds like the author's opinion? (2) Supported by at least one explicit claim? Both must be yes. The one tool that doesn't lie.

name: Upside Down Argument
what: The LSAT pattern of putting the conclusion before its support — vs school's 'conclusion goes last' rule. In more than 90% of Main Conclusion questions, the conclusion is NOT the final claim.

name: Trojan Horse Concession
what: Concessions look like agreement but are setting up the rebuttal. Words like granted / although / while / despite signal the author is about to step back briefly and then push forward harder.
source: argumentslr prototype framing + Joshua's concession content (Argument Core & Periph.pdf, A)

name: Pre-Phrase Goal
what: Pronoun-replacement protocol. Replace 'such laws' / 'this position' / 'they' with the actual reference language before stating the conclusion to yourself. Then compare to answer choices.

name: Skeptic's Ear Check
what: R&R prompt — 'What's the simplest question or challenge to this statement?' — asked after each rephrase.

name: Stegosaurus Interrogation
what: Diagnostic technique for telling premises, conclusions, and intermediate conclusions apart by asking 'How do I know that?' If the answer is 'because of this other sentence' you've found support.
source: Notes/Intermediate conclusion and Hybrid argument notes _dup1.docx (S)

name: Indicator Vault
what: The 6-category color-coded lookup for indicator words. Conclusion / Premise (FABS) / Pivot / Opinion / Opposing / Concession. Memorize them. Tattoo them if you have to.
source: Naming new for build (lifted from MC Companion's UI label) + Notes/Indicator word list .pdf (A)

name: Coach's Note
what: Per-question explanation card. Three parts: Structure map · Core move · Per-answer audit (each wrong answer tagged with a one-word verdict — 'Too strong' / 'Stay narrow' / 'Out of scope').
source: Notes/_scan_Main conclusion part 1.pdf + part 2.pdf (S, masterclass) + argumentslr framing

name: R&R Drill (Read & Restate)
what: Methodology — read piece, cover, rephrase from memory, Skeptic's Ear Check, cumulative recall, full recollection. Desktop with live mic; mobile = text-only fallback in v1.

name: X-Ray Scan
what: Toggle reveal of role colors over a quote/stimulus block. Premise/conclusion/opposing/indicator highlighted by --role-* tokens.

name: Trap Master
what: The 7-trait diagnostic system. Every wrong answer in the Simulator is tagged with one of the 7 traits — surfacing which traps a student keeps falling for.
source: Notes/Advanced- MP Question Traps_dup1.docx (A) + masterclass workbooks (S)

name: Stage-Gate Tracker
what: Universal drill wrapper. 4 stages × 5 questions, score 4/5 to unlock next, auto-save, PDF export. Drill 3.4 completion gates the Module 4 Simulator.
where: MC-DRL-3.4 (and applied to all Module 3 drills as universal frame)

name: Pronoun & Reference Library
what: The list of words to scan for in candidate conclusions: this / that / they / such / these / those / this position / such laws / etc. Replace before saying the conclusion.
source: New naming for content from Notes/Main Conclusion Synopsis.pdf (S) + role_merged.pdf (S)

name: Concession Decoder
what: Structural reveal: Concession → Pivot → Author's claim → Support. When the author appears to grant ground, brace — the actual claim follows the pivot.
source: New naming for content from Notes/Argument Core & Periph_dup1.pdf (A) + Indicator word list .pdf (A)
